1: ṣĕʿāqāh gĕdôlāh — the ‘great outcry’ invokes a specific covenant-legal tradition: the ṣĕʿāqāh is the cry of the oppressed that obliges YHWH to respond (Exodus 3:7: ‘I have heard their ṣĕʿāqāh’; Genesis 18:20-21: the ṣaʿăqat of Sodom; Job 34:28). Its appearance in Nehemiah signals that the economic crisis reaches the level of the covenant-violation YHWH promised to address in Exodus 22:23-24 (‘if you oppress them, and they cry out to me, I will surely hear their cry’). The inclusion of wĕnāšêhem (and their wives) is notable — wives rarely appear as co-agents of complaint in OT legal narratives; their inclusion signals this is a whole-household emergency, not a peripheral grievance.
2: The Hebrew text of this verse is grammatically difficult. The phrase ûḇānênû ûḇĕnōtênû raḇbîm ʾănaḥnû can be parsed as ‘our sons and daughters are many — we are’ (i.e., we are a large family, so we need grain to eat), but the LXX reads differently, suggesting some scribes understood the raḇbîm as a financial obligation rather than family size. The MKT renders it as ‘our families are large.’ The complaint is economic at its base: many mouths to feed, insufficient grain, no cash reserves. The verb niqḥāh (imperative Qal of lqḥ, to take) in the form we need to take grain is a request-verb, not a demand — the people are asking for grain access, not seizing it.
3: ʿāraḇnû — from the root ʿrb (to pledge, become surety; to be tangled/mixed). The Qal perfect ‘we have pledged’ indicates a completed action. The three items pledged — fields, vineyards, and homes (śĕdōtênû ûkarmêhem ûḇāttênû) — represent the full rural asset base: arable land, viticulture, and housing. The impetus was wĕhāreʿāḇ (and the famine) — a short-term food crisis turned into long-term land-loss through the pledging mechanism. The verb ʿrb is also the root of the word for ‘pledge/surety’ in Proverbs 17:18 and Genesis 43:9 (Judah becoming surety for Benjamin), connecting to the broader covenant-solidarity vocabulary.
4: lāwînû (we have borrowed) — root lwh, to borrow; the Qal perfect. The loan is specifically for tax payment (middāt hammelek, ‘the king’s tribute’). The Persian provincial taxation system required payment in silver (as Ezra 4:13 specifies: middāh, bĕlô, wahălāk — tribute, customs, and toll), but the community had no silver reserves and had to borrow against their land to pay. The compounding effect: fields pledged to get grain (v3) + fields again to borrow silver for taxes (v4) + fields already mortgaged unable to generate income to repay = systematic debt-slavery. The karmênû ûśĕdōtênû (our vineyards and fields) appears here too — the same assets already pledged in v3 are now also pledged for the tax debt.
5:
kĕḇaśar ʾaḥêhem bĕśārênû (as our brothers’ flesh is our flesh) — the kinship-solidarity formula using bāśār (flesh/body) as the marker of shared humanity and covenant obligation. In covenant contexts, ‘one flesh’ signals the strongest bond (Genesis 2:24; Judges 9:2). The argument is: we are blood-kin, yet our children face what their children do not face.

kōḇĕšîm (from kbš, to subdue, to bring under foot) — the participle is strong; the root is used of subduing enemies in battle (Numbers 32:22,29) and of subduing the earth in Genesis 1:28. Applied to children being ‘subdued’ into slavery by their own kinsmen, the word is a covenant-violation term at its most severe. The MKT renders it ‘reduce... to slavery,’ but the image is more violent than mere labor: it is conquest-language applied to covenant kin.
6: wayyiḥar lî mĕʾōd — literally ‘it burned greatly for me.’ The idiom uses the root ḥārāh (to burn, to become hot) with the dative (lî) rather than the more common ḥārāh ʾappô (his nose burned = he was angry). The construction without ʾap (nose) but with lî (to me) is unusual — it may indicate an internalized fury rather than an outward expression. The adverb mĕʾōd (very much, greatly) intensifies the emotional state. The verse is a single, stark sentence after the long complaint-speeches of vv2-5, functioning as the narrator’s pivot toward action.
7:
wayyimmalek lĕḇābî ʿālay — the Hithpoel of mlk (to be king, to take counsel) with ‘my heart’ as subject is a rare construction meaning to deliberate inwardly or consult with oneself. The form expresses a reflexive consultation — Nehemiah’s heart deliberated over him, advising him before he acted. The pause between anger (v6) and action is itself significant.

wāʾrîḇāh — the Qal imperfect of ryb (to contend, to dispute at law, to bring a lawsuit). The verb is used throughout the prophets for YHWH’s covenant lawsuit against Israel (Hosea 4:1; Micah 6:2; Jeremiah 2:9) and for legal contests between parties (Deuteronomy 17:8; Nehemiah 19:17). The MKT ‘brought a formal charge’ captures the legal-contest nuance better than ‘rebuked.’ Nehemiah is not merely expressing displeasure — he is initiating a legal process.
8:
qānîtî (I bought) — root qnh (to buy, acquire, possess). The same root underlies the kinsman-redeemer (gōʾēl) concept, though Nehemiah does not use the gōʾēl term here. The action of buying back Jewish kin from foreign nations (ĕlgôyim) directly invokes Leviticus 25:47-55 (the redemption of kin sold to a foreigner). The rhetorical move: Nehemiah’s community spent resources to redeem exiles from foreign bondage; the nobles are now re-enslaving those same kin to get those resources back. The logic is self-defeating for covenant community integrity.

gam-hēm tikmĕrû — ‘so that they can be sold to us?’ The root mkr (to sell) in the Niphal/Qal; the question is biting: are you going to make us buy them back again?
9:
lēleket bĕyirʾat ʾĕlōhênû — the infinitive construct of hlk (to walk) with bĕyirʾat (in the fear of) is the Deuteronomic lifestyle-theology phrase; walking in the fear of God is the Hebrew equivalent of ‘living faithfully.’ The bĕ-preposition (in/within the fear of God) places fear of God as the sphere of action, not merely an attitude. The argument is not primarily economic but covenantal: economic justice is what the fear of God looks like.

ḥerpat haggôyim — the reproach of the nations; the community’s economic misconduct damages the covenant people’s testimony before their neighbors. The word ḥerpāh (reproach, shame) is the honor-vocabulary that recurs across Nehemiah (Nehemiah 1:3; Nehemiah 2:17; Nehemiah 3:36; Nehemiah 5:9) — the whole wall-building project is about removing the ḥerpāh that Jerusalem’s ruined state creates.
10: naʿăzĕḇāh nāʾ — the cohortative (we should/let us) + nāʾ (particle of entreaty, please) makes Nehemiah’s demand into a communal request: ‘please, let us stop charging interest.’ The particle nāʾ softens the tone and invites collaborative action rather than imposing compliance. This is notable: Nehemiah does not command the nobles but invites them to join a communal standard he himself follows. The maššāʾ (burden, loan, interest) carries a double meaning — the financial loan and the social burden that the lending creates.
11: maʾat hakkesep — translated as ‘one-percent interest’ in many versions, but the exact rate is debated. The word maʾat can mean a hundredth (1%) or simply ‘a portion’ without specifying the rate. The LXX and Vulgate also render the rate differently. The MKT ‘one-percent interest’ follows the most common scholarly reading but with the caveat that the original may simply mean the prevailing interest rate (which in the ANE was often 20-25% annually for grain and 33% for silver). The demand to restore (hāšîḇû, Hiphil imperative of šûḇ) is the covenant-restoration verb — the same word used for Israel’s return to YHWH; here it’s economic restitution using the repentance-vocabulary.
12: wayyōʾmĕrû (they said) followed by nāšîḇ (we will restore) and lōʾ-nĕḇaqqēš (we will not seek) — three consecutive cohortatives/imperfects expressing commitment. Nehemiah’s immediate calling of the priests (hakkōhănîm) to administer the oath shows he did not trust verbal commitment alone. The yiššāḇēʿûm (he made them swear) is the Hiphil of šbʿ — the causative, meaning Nehemiah caused the nobles to swear by a formalized oath, which created a legally binding obligation in Israelite law (Leviticus 5:4; Num 30).
13:
ʾeṯ-ḥōṣnî — the fold or front of a garment used as a carrying-pouch; nāʿar (to shake out) creates an enacted curse-gesture: as the garment-fold is shaken empty, so let YHWH empty every person who breaks this oath. The enacted curse or oath-gesture is a well-attested ANE practice — the covenant-ceremony involving the cutting of animals in Gen 15 or the oath-gestures in Jeremiah 34:18-19 (walking between the cut halves of the animal) used symbolic acts to make the oath’s consequences vivid.

wayyehî kaddāḇār hazzeh (let it be so/may it be done) — the community’s ratification; the word kaddāḇār is ‘like this word/thing,’ an affirmation of the enacted curse. The verb wayhallĕlû (they praised YHWH) reflects the covenant-affirmation that follows the oath — worship as the seal of the legal commitment.
14:
peḥat yĕhûdāh — the Persian loanword peḥāh (governor, from Old Persian pitiḫa, ‘representative’) combined with the province name; the title establishes Nehemiah’s administrative authority. The twelve-year calculation (‘from the twentieth to the thirty-second year of King Artaxerxes’) = 445-433 BCE — the precise Persian regnal-year dating is characteristic of the Nehemiah memoir’s documentary style.

ʾet-leḥem happeḥāh lōʾ ʾāḵaltî — ‘the food of the governor I did not eat’ — the word order is emphatic (object first, then subject and verb), stressing the governor’s bread specifically that Nehemiah refused. The governor’s bread (leḥem happeḥāh) refers to the tax-in-kind that previous governors had exacted for their personal table — essentially a food-tribute on top of the silver taxes.
15:
wayyikbĕdû ʿal-hāʿām — ‘they were heavy/burdensome upon the people’ — the verb kāḇad in the Qal (to be heavy) is the standard word for oppressive governance throughout the OT (cf. 1 Samuel 5:6,11; 2 Samuel 13:25). The specific figure of ʾarbaʿîm šeqel kesep (forty shekels of silver) as the daily food-and-wine allocation appears to be a tax-in-kind that previous governors extracted. Even their servants (naʿărêhem) exercised dominion (šālĕṭû, root šlṭ = to have dominion/authority) over the people — the governor’s servants had derivative power over the population.

mirrāʾat ʾĕlōhîm lōʾ ʿāśîtî kēn — ‘but because of the fear of God, I did not act thus’ — the yirʾat ʾĕlōhîm (fear of God) from v9 returns as the stated motivation for Nehemiah’s fiscal restraint. The fear of God is not merely a theological principle but the practical driver of administrative behavior.
16: waḥazaqtî ûmal‘aḵtî — ‘I gave myself fully to the work’ (literally ‘I strengthened myself in/on the work’) — the verb ḥzq in the Hithpael (reflexive-intensive) means to strengthen oneself; the reflexive use indicates Nehemiah’s active self-application to the wall-building. The emphatic addition lōʾ qānîtî śādeh (I did not buy any land) is significant: land-acquisition by officials using their administrative position was a common form of corruption; Nehemiah explicitly refused it.
17: wĕhayĕhûdîm wĕhaśśĕgānîm — ‘the Jewish officials’ — śāgān (official/prefect) is an Akkadian loanword appearing across the late OT literature (Ezra, Nehemiah, Jer, Ezek) for civil officials; distinct from the śārîm (nobles) and ḥōrîm (freemen). The 150 officials at Nehemiah’s daily table, plus visitors from surrounding nations, is a substantial hospitality obligation — his point is that he bore the full cost from his personal resources rather than from tax revenue.
18: The daily table provision — one ox (šôr ʾeḥād), six prime sheep (ṣōʾn bĕrûrôt šēš), birds, and wine every ten days — is an elaborate itemization that mirrors ancient Near Eastern administrative records of daily provisions for royal officials. The emphasis on wine every ten days (bĕʿaśôr yāmîm) specifies a particular luxury portion. bĕkol-zōʾt (despite all this) — the adversative conjunction highlighting the contrast: all this expense, yet lōʾ bāqqaštî ʾet-leḥem happeḥāh (I did not demand the governor’s food allowance) — the formal right he had but voluntarily waived.
19: zĕḵārāh-lî ʾĕlōhay lĕṭôḇāh — the first of five ‘remember me’ prayers in Nehemiah (Nehemiah 5:19; Nehemiah 6:14; Nehemiah 13:14; Nehemiah 13:22; Nehemiah 13:31). The Qal imperative zĕḵār (remember) addressed directly to God is not a demand but a covenantal appeal — YHWH’s remembering is his saving attention to the covenant (Genesis 8:1: ‘God remembered Noah’; 1 Samuel 1:19: ‘YHWH remembered Hannah’). The dative lî (for me) specifies the direction of the remembrance; lĕṭôḇāh (for good/as a good thing) specifies its quality — favorable, beneficent remembering. The phrase ʾăšer ʿāśîtî ʿal-hāʿām hazzeh (all I have done for this people) grounds the petition in the record of covenant service rather than in a claim of merit.
